reviews.review_msg: I really enjoyed Richard's lectures. He keeps things light and fun, which is always welcome during the 3 hour lectures. The material covered is interesting, it doesn't go as deep into the weeds as some classes, but instead covers a wide range of topics related to attention.

This was my second class with Richard, and like 221, I appreciated the format of 2 take-home exams and 5 quizzes. However, the quiz questions were overly specific, with just one minute to answer, and the lengthy wording often consumed that time. Also, the lack of a grading rubric for exams made it difficult to know how to improve for the next exam.

Dr. Wright is great. He's passionate about cognitive psych and tries his best to make the lectures interesting. Some of the take home exam questions are pretty vague and require a lot of research but overall they were fair. The quizzes are usually pretty simple but I found the last one challenging. Average was pretty consistently 75%.

Great class. To do well in this course you MUST attend lectures and read all of the chapters - which he provides to you for free, as he is still writing the textbook. Aside from the midterm and final, there are 3 thought questions worth 15% total. Exams are all multiple choice, but can be difficult if you don't know the material

I had no idea what to study for exams, and the lectures were hard to follow. He mainly tested us on memorizing terms from the handouts he gave, however he's way better than Spalek (psyc 221). Oh, and he hates it when someone asks questions in class, he doesn't have an answer 99% of the time and gets angry if people want to engage in a discussion.

He is very passionate about what he is teaching. The material is tough and very neuroscience based, which some psychology students will find difficult. He is really nice and willing to help outside of class hours, if you ask for it. Does not really like a lot of questions during lectures. 85% exam based, and the exams are hard!
Tests are quite difficult, no powerpoints posted online. assignments are easy, depends on TM. would be helpful if notes were posted online however class is doable.
exams are multiple choice and not too bad, his lectures seen disorganized and he seems like he doesnt really know what he is talking about.
I don't think he even believes some of the things he teaches.  He's really uninspired about it.

Worst. Class. Ever.
 
 Joan is a terrible professor... I really don't know how she is teaching at this level. I've had Ph.D. students teach better than her. All she does is go up to the front of the class, open her book and read. She also goes very fast.
 
 The class it self isn't too hard though. Skip the lectures and concentrate on the book.
Great prof. Super approachable.
Great prof, great material, fair exams

His lectures are very interesting and he keeps it lighthearted. The midterm exam was so easy he used questions he provided in the sample midterm for us beforehand and a lot of it was based on the lecture.

This class is pretty tough. Lots of readings but he provides good lecture slides. Midterm and final are really hard, but he lets you bring in one cheat sheet for the mid term and two cheat sheets for the final.  The legal research assignment and the two other assignments plus tutorial participation should help boost your mark though
This prof really cares about his student's doing well.  Great guy to take the class from, made it interesting and relevant
Super approachable, helpful, clear, organized, and most of all SO FUN! It would be a mistake to leave SFU without taking a course with her first. The gem of the criminology department :)
Okay

Lectures were organized, interesting, and extremely clear. Tutorials require participation to earn the full marks, otherwise you'll only get half for showing up. Exams were tough and based on time management! He's very knowledgeable and insightful and has humor. I would take this class again. If you work hard, you'll do fine.
Smart and fun with a very clear teaching style, which is hot in itself.  Smokin' prof!

You guys need to clam down, she is actually one of the successful and most respected member at SFU. She is on several executive committee such as CMC, NSERC and etc. I guess we are sometimes too caught on teaching skills and grades, but the bigger picture is different. In real life, no one will be teaching you, you need to learn by yourselves.
i found this guy to be very smart, patient, and helpful. most people are afraid to approach him because he looks scary, but he's really nice once you approach him...
